explanation: The natural language statement "the control-traffic message that must be sent only after querying the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components" implies a strict temporal ordering: the action of sending the control-traffic message cannot occur unless both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components have been queried previously or concurrently. The LTL formula `G (P -> F (Q U P))` is used to express "P only after Q" (non-strict precedence). Here, P represents `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` (the control-traffic message being sent), and Q represents `(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)` (both components being queried). The formula states that globally, if `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` occurs, then it must be true that eventually `(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)` holds until `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` holds. This means that by the time the control-traffic message is sent, both querying actions must have occurred. The phrase "This property holds for every DM_i" is covered by the global (G) operator and the general nature of the atomic propositions which apply to any DM_i.

The natural language statement specifies a condition for sending a control-traffic message: it 'must be sent only after querying the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components'. This implies a safety property, meaning the message should never be sent without the specified queries being performed. The LTL formula `G (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))` captures this requirement. It states that Globally (`G`), whenever a `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` occurs, it must be true that both `local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set` and `local_node_queried_duplicate_set` are also true at that exact same moment. This interpretation of 'only after' includes concurrency, meaning the enabling condition (the queries) must be met at the moment the action (sending the message) occurs. This is a standard way to express preconditions or enabling conditions in LTL for system behavior.

The property states that a 'control-traffic message must be sent only after querying the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components'. This means that if a control-traffic message is sent, then the querying of both MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set must have occurred previously or concurrently. This is captured by the LTL pattern G(A -> ((!A) U B) | B), where A is the event that must happen only after B, and B is the prerequisite event. In this case, A is 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi', and B is the conjunction of 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set'. The 'G' (globally) operator ensures that this condition holds throughout the execution for every DM_i.

This property ensures that a 'controlTraffic' message is only sent after both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set components have been queried. The LTL formula G(C -> (Q U C)) captures this by stating: Globally (G), if 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' (C) becomes true, then it must be the case that '(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)' (Q) was true at some point in the past or is true at the current moment, and C itself was false in all states strictly between the point where Q was true and the current moment when C becomes true. This precisely models the 'only after' condition, ensuring that C never happens unless Q has preceded or coincided with it.

This LTL property ensures that a 'controlTraffic message' (represented by `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi`, let's call it P) is sent only after the 'MPR Selector Set' and 'Duplicate Set' components have both been queried (represented by `local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set`, let's call their conjunction Q). The phrase "only after" implies that if P occurs, then Q must have occurred at some point prior to or at the same time as P.

The formula `G ( ! ( (!Q) U P ) )` captures this requirement:
- `G(...)`: The property must hold globally at all times, reflecting "This property holds for every DM_i."
- `(!Q) U P`: This subformula asserts that "not Q" remains true until P becomes true. This describes a scenario where Q has not occurred yet, and P is about to occur (or has just occurred).
- `!((!Q) U P)`: This negates the above subformula. It states that it is forbidden for `!Q` to remain true until `P` occurs. Therefore, if `P` does occur, it must imply that the condition `(!Q) U P` was false. This can only happen if, at some point before `P` occurred (or at the exact moment `P` occurred), `Q` was true. This directly enforces the "only after" requirement, ensuring that the control-traffic message is sent only when the queries have already taken place.

The property states that a 'controlTraffic message' (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) must be sent exclusively after both the 'MPR Selector Set' (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) and 'Duplicate Set' (local_node_queried_duplicate_set) have been queried. The LTL formula `G (A -> (!A U B))` captures the 'A only after B' requirement. In this specific case, 'A' is `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi`, and 'B' is the conjunction of the two queries: `(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)`. The `G` (Globally) operator ensures this condition holds at all times. The `U` (Until) operator ensures that `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` cannot be true (`!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi`) until `(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)` becomes true. If `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` ever occurs, it implies that the 'until' condition must have been met by `(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)` becoming true at some point in the past, and `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` must not have occurred during the interval leading up to that point.

The property states that a 'controlTraffic message' (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) must only be sent after 'querying the MPR Selector Set' (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) AND 'querying the Duplicate Set components' (local_node_queried_duplicate_set). This means that at any point in time, if a control-traffic message is about to be sent, both MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set must have already been queried. This can be expressed using the 'Until' (U) operator.

The LTL formula G((!A) U B) is used to express "A occurs only after B" or "B must precede A". In this formula:
- A is 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi'.
- B is '(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)'.

The formula `G((!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) U (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))` means: Globally (G), it must always be the case that 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' is false (!controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) Until (U) '(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)' becomes true. This correctly enforces that the control-traffic message cannot be sent before both querying actions have occurred. If the querying actions never occur, then the control-traffic message can also never be sent, which aligns with the "only after" condition.

This LTL property asserts that it is always true that if a control-traffic message is sent (represented by 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi'), then concurrently, both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components must have been queried (represented by 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set'). The natural language phrase "only after" implies a temporal ordering where the querying must precede or coincide with the message being sent. The natural language statement "the control-traffic message that must be sent only after querying the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components" implies a strict temporal order: if a control-traffic message is sent, then the querying of the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set must have happened before or at the same time. The LTL formula captures this using the `G` (Globally) and `V` (Release) operators. Let `A` be `controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi` and `B` be `(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)`. The expression `G (A -> (B V A))` means: Globally, if `A` occurs, then `B` must have been true at some point before or concurrently with `A`. The `B V A` (B releases A) ensures that if A happens, it's not the case that B was continuously false until A. This implies B must have been true at some point before or at the occurrence of A, satisfying the 'only after' condition.

The natural language statement "the control-traffic message that must be sent only after querying the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components" implies a strict temporal ordering: the control-traffic message cannot be sent unless both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components have been queried. This is a safety property ensuring a precondition is met. The LTL formula G(P -> !( (!Q) U P )) is used to express "P only after Q". Here, 'P' is 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi', and 'Q' is the conjunction of the two queries: '(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)'. The formula states that globally, if a control-traffic message is sent, then it must not be the case that the queries (Q) have been continuously false until the message was sent. This effectively means that Q must have occurred at some point on or before the message (P) is sent, thus capturing the "only after" requirement. If Q never occurs, then P can never occur without violating the property.
